title: Error when running sample app - "Internal error" "The operation could not be completed"
body:
I've downloaded their sample app called 'CameraAccess'. I then set up my apple team credentials and was able to build and deploy to the ipad. When I start the CameraAccess app it shows a button that says "Connect my glasses". There is a small message just above the button that says "You'll be redirected to the Meta AI app to confirm your connection." When I press the button, the meta ai app opens and I see my device (glasses) listed in the background. But then I get a pop up message that says "Internal error" "The operation could not be completed" My only option is to press ok. But then, I'm just waiting in the Meta AI app. There is no other information or error messages.

I believe this is during the "registration" stage, but the error offers no help.

Any advice?

All devices are up to date and latest.  Wearables are gen 1 wayfarers.

comments.author: cassiomachado
comments.body: Hi @John-Doolan this seems related to enabling the Developer Mode. Please follow this step in our documentation: https://wearables.developer.meta.com/docs/getting-started-toolkit#enable-developer-mode-in-the-meta-ai-app

title: [iOS] Camera Stream Stops Immediately After Start - FigApplicationStateMonitor Error -19431
body:
When using the Meta Wearables DAT SDK (v0.2.1) on iOS, the camera stream starts successfully but immediately stops with FigApplicationStateMonitor error -19431, preventing any photo capture functionality.

Environment
SDK Version: meta-wearables-dat-ios v0.2.1
Platform: iOS
Device: iPhone
iOS Version: Latest
Xcode Version: Latest
Smartglasses: Ray-Ban Meta
Integration: Swift/SwiftUI
Steps to Reproduce
Configure SDK with Wearables.configure()
Complete registration flow via startRegistration() and Meta View app
Request and grant all permissions (Camera, Microphone, SDK camera permission)
Create StreamSession and set up listeners
Call streamSession.start()
Observe session state changes
Expected Behavior
Session transitions: starting → streaming (remains active)
User can capture photos via streamSession.capturePhoto(format: .jpeg)
Stream continues until explicitly stopped
Actual Behavior
Session transitions: starting → streaming → stopping → stopped
Stream stops within milliseconds of reaching streaming state
iOS system error appears in console: FigApplicationStateMonitor >>>> signalled err=-19431
Console Output
🔄 Session state changed: starting
▶️ Session STARTING
🔄 Session state changed: streaming
✅ Session STREAMING - camera feed active!
<<<< FigApplicationStateMonitor >>>> signalled err=-19431 at <>:474
<<<< FigApplicationStateMonitor >>>> signalled err=-19431 at <>:474
<<<< FigApplicationStateMonitor >>>> signalled err=-19431 at <>:474
🔄 Session state changed: stopping
⏸️ Session STOPPING
🔄 Session state changed: stopped
⏹️ Session STOPPED
Code Sample
Setup & Configuration
swift
import MWDATCore
import MWDATCamera
import AVFoundation

class SmartglassesManager: ObservableObject {
    private var wearables: Wearables?
    private var streamSession: StreamSession?
    
    init() {
        do {
            try Wearables.configure()
            self.wearables = Wearables.shared
            setupSession()
        } catch {
            print("Failed to configure: \(error)")
        }
    }
    
    private func setupSession() {
        guard let wearables = wearables else { return }
        streamSession = wearables.createStreamSession()
        
        // Listen for state changes
        streamSession?.statePublisher.listen { state in
            print("Session state: \(state)")
        }
    }
}
Audio Session Configuration
swift
func startStreaming() async throws {
    // Configure iOS audio session
    let audioSession = AVAudioSession.sharedInstance()
    try audioSession.setCategory(.playAndRecord, 
                                 mode: .videoChat, 
                                 options: [.allowBluetooth, .defaultToSpeaker])
    try audioSession.setActive(true)
    
    // Request iOS permissions
    let cameraGranted = await AVCaptureDevice.requestAccess(for: .video)
    let audioGranted = await AVAudioSession.sharedInstance().requestRecordPermission()
    
    // Request SDK permission
    let status = try await wearables.checkPermissionStatus(.camera)
    if status != .granted {
        _ = try await wearables.requestPermission(.camera)
    }
    
    // Start stream
    await streamSession?.start()  // ← Stops immediately after this
}
Info.plist Configuration
xml
<key>MWDAT</key>
<dict>
    <key>AppId</key>
    <string>[Your App ID]</string>
    <key>AppSecret</key>
    <string>[Your App Secret]</string>
</dict>

<key>UIBackgroundModes</key>
<array>
    <string>audio</string>
    <string>external-accessory</string>
</array>

<key>NSCameraUsageDescription</key>
<string>Camera access for smartglasses streaming</string>

<key>NSMicrophoneUsageDescription</key>
<string>Microphone access for smartglasses audio</string>

<key>NSBluetoothAlwaysUsageDescription</key>
<string>Bluetooth for Ray-Ban Meta glasses connection</string>

<key>UISupportedExternalAccessoryProtocols</key>
<array>
    <string>com.facebook.wearable</string>
</array>

<key>LSApplicationQueriesSchemes</key>
<array>
    <string>meta-view</string>
</array>
What We've Tried
iOS Configuration
✅ Proper audio session setup (.playAndRecord, .videoChat)
✅ Bluetooth audio options (.allowBluetooth, .defaultToSpeaker)
✅ Background modes enabled (audio, external-accessory)
✅ All required Info.plist keys configured
✅ Scene phase monitoring (app stays in foreground)
Presentation
✅ Tried .sheet presentation
✅ Tried .fullScreenCover presentation
✅ Both result in same error
Permission Flow
✅ Request iOS camera permission first
✅ Request iOS microphone permission
✅ Then request SDK camera permission
✅ All permissions granted successfully
Concurrency
✅ Added guard to prevent duplicate start() calls
✅ Ensured only one streaming attempt at a time
None of these solutions prevent the error.

Analysis
FigApplicationStateMonitor Error -19431
This is an iOS system error that typically means:

App lost foreground/active status
Camera session was interrupted by system
Another process claimed camera access
However:

Our app is in foreground (verified with scene phase monitoring)
No other apps are using camera
Error occurs immediately after successful stream start
Happens consistently on every attempt
Hypothesis
This appears to be an SDK-internal issue where:

The SDK successfully starts the camera stream
Something in the SDK or iOS interaction causes the system to interrupt the session
The interruption happens before any frames can be captured
Questions
Is this a known issue with iOS integration?
Does the official CameraAccess sample app work correctly on iOS?
Are there additional iOS-specific requirements not documented?
Is there a recommended workaround?
Is this fixed in a newer SDK version?
Comparison with Sample App
We've reviewed the official CameraAccess sample app and implemented the same patterns:

Registration flow
Permission handling
Session setup
State listeners
The main difference is our app is a full SwiftUI app, while the sample uses UIKit patterns. Could this be related?

Impact
Critical: Completely blocks camera streaming functionality
Cannot capture photos from Ray-Ban Meta glasses
Makes the SDK unusable for our use case
Workaround Needed
Is there a recommended approach to:

Keep the stream alive after it starts?
Prevent the FigApplicationStateMonitor interruption?
Detect and auto-restart the stream?
Additional Information
Registration and connection to glasses works perfectly
All permissions are granted correctly
The stream does reach streaming state before stopping
No errors from SDK itself, only iOS system error
Bluetooth connection remains stable
Environment Details

Swift Package Manager integration
SwiftUI-based app
Using async/await for SDK calls
Thank you for any guidance on resolving this issue!

title: Oakley Meta Vanguard streaming support status?
body:
I'm trying to use the DAT SDK (v0.2.1) with Oakley Meta Vanguard glasses. The device registers successfully and shows as connected (linkState: connected), but streaming doesn't work:

- Using AutoDeviceSelector: Stream stays stuck at waitingForDevice
- Using SpecificDeviceSelector with the device ID: Stream moves to starting but then times out

[STREAM] Device: 02LL, linkState: connected
[STREAM] State: starting
[STREAM] Error: timeout

I noticed DeviceType.oakleyMetaVanguard exists in the SDK enum, so the SDK is aware of the device.

Questions:
1. Is Vanguard streaming currently supported in the SDK?
2. If not, is there a timeline for when support will be added?
3. Is there a workaround to get streaming working with Vanguard in the meantime?

Thanks!

comments.author: metadavithom
comments.body:
Hi, unfortunately we did not launch with Oakley Meta Vanguard support. See our list of [supported devices here](https://wearables.developer.meta.com/docs/getting-started-toolkit/#hardware-requirements).

The SDK requires a future version of the firmware, v20 or higher. When that rolls out for Vanguard glasses, we will add support in the SDK, but unfortunately in the meantime you will need to use other devices or the [Mock Device Kit](https://wearables.developer.meta.com/docs/mock-device-kit)

title: Request for display SDK
body:
Hello team,

Thank you for making this project available — the work behind it is truly appreciated.

I understand that the initial SDK does not include display access for the Meta Ray-Ban display glasses. I would like to submit a feature request for exposing APIs that allow developers to display things on it. This capability would greatly expand what developers can build with the device.

If this is planned or under consideration, any updates would be appreciated.

Thank you again for your efforts.

Best,
Wenjin

comments.author: metadavithom
comments.body:
Thank you for your interest in the SDK - really exciting to see what people create.

Meta Ray Ban Display support, both with the current SDK and with a display API is definitely something we want to add in future, but I cannot give a precise timeline at this moment. I hope we will have more information for you soon.

comments.author: kkai
comments.body:
Hello team,

I would also like to express my support for this request.

Access to the display would open up many interesting possibilities for developers and researchers, and would significantly expand the kinds of applications that can be built for the Meta Ray-Ban display glasses.

I understand that you may not be able to share exact dates yet, but I was wondering whether there are any roadmap updates you can share regarding display API support. Even a rough indication of whether this is a near-term or longer-term priority would be very helpful for planning.

Thank you again for your work on the SDK and for engaging with the community.

Best,

Kai

title: Glasses hardware button does not trigger photo capture during streaming (Meta Gen 2 Wayfarers)
body:
## Title

**Glasses hardware button does not trigger photo capture during streaming (Meta Gen 2 Wayfarers)**

## Description

The physical camera button on Meta Gen 2 Wayfarers does not trigger photo capture when a streaming session is active, even though the stream is in `.streaming` state and the `photoDataPublisher` listener is registered.

### Expected Behavior

Pressing the physical camera button on the glasses during an active streaming session should trigger the `photoDataPublisher` listener and capture a photo.

### Actual Behavior

- Physical glasses button: does not trigger photo capture
- App button (`streamSession.capturePhoto()`): works correctly

### Steps to Reproduce

1. Start streaming session with `StreamSession` (state becomes `.streaming`)
2. Register `photoDataPublisher` listener
3. Press physical camera button on glasses → no photo captured
4. Press app camera button → photo captured successfully

### Code

```swift
photoDataListenerToken = streamSession.photoDataPublisher.listen { photoData in
  print("Photo captured!") // Only fires for app button, not glasses button
}

// This works:
streamSession.capturePhoto(format: .jpeg) // ✅

// This doesn't work:
// Pressing physical button on glasses // ❌
```

### Environment

- **SDK Version**: Meta Wearables DAT iOS v0.2.1
- **Device**: Meta Gen 2 Wayfarers
- **Platform**: iOS

### Question

Is this expected behavior? The SDK documentation suggests `photoDataPublisher` should capture photos. 

### Impact

This limits the SDK's usefulness for hands-free workflows. We need to use the glasses button directly on the glasses device, not rely on the iPhone screen. If the glasses button doesn't work during streaming, we cannot achieve a true hands-free experience. 

**Note:** Happy to help build functionality, but the SDK appears to be a compiled binary, so I can't submit a PR. How can I contribute?

comments.author: cassiomachado
comments.body:
Hello @omnia-tom thank you for raising this scenario, however this is the expected behavior and only actions on your mobile app would capture photo or video. They will not be triggered by the glasses button (called capture button).

More context: the Wearables Device Access Toolkit runs work inside sessions (detailed here: https://wearables.developer.meta.com/docs/lifecycle-events). Single press and long press of the capture button won't be available during those sessions.

Hope this was helpful. Please let us know if you have seen different information anywhere so that we can clarify that.

Thank you.

comments.author: omnia-tom
comments.body:
Hi @cassiomachado - are there any alternative ways to make something like this work? 

Essentially, my team and I would like to send either a short video or photo to one of our video/photo processing APIs. In order to have a useful experience, we were hoping for the photo to be sent when we click on the capture button on the glasses without having the phone open. 

Otherwise, this highly limits what we can build for these glasses unfortunately.

comments.author: samsldev
comments.body: I was also thinking about using this feature. If you could at least reconsider/rethink it, I would appreciate it, as this could really limit the concept of "Hands-Free" photo/video apps/applications. This kind of creates the need to use a cell phone and increases the barrier to ease of use in everyday life (just a thought, I was following this issue, thank you for the response).

comments.author: goohooh
comments.body: This policy is ridiculous. “Hands-free without streaming”? So hands-free works for everything but streaming? That doesn’t make any sense.

Hope this was helpful. Please let us know if you have seen different information anywhere so that we can clarify that.

Thank you.

The physical camera button on Meta Gen 2 Wayfarers does not trigger photo capture when a streaming session is active, even though the stream is in `.streaming` state and the `photoDataPublisher` listener is registered.

### Expected Behavior

Pressing the physical camera button on the glasses during an active streaming session should trigger the `photoDataPublisher` listener and capture a photo.

### Actual Behavior

- Physical glasses button: does not trigger photo capture
- App button (`streamSession.capturePhoto()`): works correctly

### Steps to Reproduce

1. Start streaming session with `StreamSession` (state becomes `.streaming`)
2. Register `photoDataPublisher` listener
3. Press physical camera button on glasses → no photo captured
4. Press app camera button → photo captured successfully

### Code

```swift
photoDataListenerToken = streamSession.photoDataPublisher.listen { photoData in
  print("Photo captured!") // Only fires for app button, not glasses button
}

// This works:
streamSession.capturePhoto(format: .jpeg) // ✅

// This doesn't work:
// Pressing physical button on glasses // ❌
```

### Environment

- **SDK Version**: Meta Wearables DAT iOS v0.2.1
- **Device**: Meta Gen 2 Wayfarers
- **Platform**: iOS

### Question

Is this expected behavior? The SDK documentation suggests `photoDataPublisher` should capture photos. 

### Impact

This limits the SDK's usefulness for hands-free workflows. We need to use the glasses button directly on the glasses device, not rely on the iPhone screen. If the glasses button doesn't work during streaming, we cannot achieve a true hands-free experience. 

**Note:** Happy to help build functionality, but the SDK appears to be a compiled binary, so I can't submit a PR. How can I contribute?
RESOLUTION: Hello @omnia-tom thank you for raising this scenario, however this is the expected behavior and only actions on your mobile app would capture photo or video. They will not be triggered by the glasses button (called capture button).

More context: the Wearables Device Access Toolkit runs work inside sessions (detailed here: https://wearables.developer.meta.com/docs/lifecycle-events). Single press and long press of the capture button won't be available during those sessions.

Hope this was helpful. Please let us know if you have seen different information anywhere so that we can clarify that.

Thank you.
COMMENTS: Hi @cassiomachado - are there any alternative ways to make something like this work? 

Essentially, my team and I would like to send either a short video or photo to one of our video/photo processing APIs. In order to have a useful experience, we were hoping for the photo to be sent when we click on the capture button on the glasses without having the phone open. 

Otherwise, this highly limits what we can build for these glasses unfortunately.  I was also thinking about using this feature. If you could at least reconsider/rethink it, I would appreciate it, as this could really limit the concept of "Hands-Free" photo/video apps/applications. This kind of creates the need to use a cell phone and increases the barrier to ease of use in everyday life (just a thought, I was following this issue, thank you for the response). This policy is ridiculous. “Hands-free without streaming”? So hands-free works for everything but streaming? That doesn’t make any sense.

title: [Bug]: Priority Inversion and Hang due to MockDeviceKit.shared.pairRaybanMeta()
body: ## Description Calling `MockDeviceKit.shared.pairRaybanMeta()` results in a noticeable hang during test execution and triggers a runtime "purple" warning" in Xcode's, indicating a priority inversion: > Thread running at User-interactive quality-of-service class waiting on a lower QoS thread running at Default quality-of-service class. Investigate ways to avoid priority inversions Code snippet: ```Swift import XCTest import MWDATMockDevice @MainActor class MyTest: XCTestCase { override func setUp() async throws { try await super.setUp() let _ = MockDeviceKit.shared.pairRaybanMeta() } func test() async throws {} } ``` ## Expected Behavior The mock device should pair without blocking the Main Thread and without triggering priority inversion warnings. ## Actual Behavior Runtime purple warning ## Performance Sometimes hangs ## Environment MetaWearablesDAT: `0.3.0` (Reproducible on `0.2.1`) Swift: `5` Xcode: `26.1.1` Device: physical iPhone 13 and iPhone17 simulator iOS: `26` ## Attempted fixes that failed: - Moving the call to a detached task - Moving the call in the test - Ensuring it's called once and only once - Moving the call outside the test suite

TITLE: [Bug]: Priority Inversion and Hang due to MockDeviceKit.shared.pairRaybanMeta()
PROBLEM: ## Description



Calling `MockDeviceKit.shared.pairRaybanMeta()` results in a noticeable hang during test execution and triggers a runtime "purple" warning" in Xcode's, indicating a priority inversion:

> Thread running at User-interactive quality-of-service class waiting on a lower QoS thread running at Default quality-of-service class. Investigate ways to avoid priority inversions

Code snippet:
```Swift
import XCTest
import MWDATMockDevice

@MainActor
class MyTest: XCTestCase {
   
  override func setUp() async throws {
    try await super.setUp()
    let _ = MockDeviceKit.shared.pairRaybanMeta()
  }
     
  func test() async throws {}
}
```

## Expected Behavior
The mock device should pair without blocking the Main Thread and without triggering priority inversion warnings.

## Actual Behavior
Runtime purple warning

## Performance
Sometimes hangs

## Environment
MetaWearablesDAT: `0.3.0` (Reproducible on `0.2.1`)
Swift: `5`
Xcode: `26.1.1`
Device: physical iPhone 13 and iPhone17 simulator
iOS: `26`

## Attempted fixes that failed:
- Moving the call to a detached task
- Moving the call in the test
- Ensuring it's called once and only once
- Moving the call outside the test suite

Our platform is not yet open for submissions to Meta, which in turn would unblock the release to the App Store or Play Store. We will support submission and publishing later in 2026, and will notify everyone here on our repos and other media. 

In the meantime, a few options:
1. If you'd like to distribute to test users in the meantime, you can follow our instructions for setting up release channels [here](https://wearables.developer.meta.com/docs/set-up-release-channels). We support up to 100 test users per channel.

3. If you haven't yet, you can also submit an interest form with more details: https://developers.meta.com/wearables/notify/

Thank you, we are closing the issue now, and will update as we open up for submissions soon.

title: Devices Stream() returns empty despite glasses connected via Bluetooth HFP and successful registration.
body:
Hi Meta Wearables team, 

I'm Anthony Chavez, developer of VoiceSwap - a voice-activated crypto payments app for Meta Ray-Ban glasses. We're experiencing an issue where devicesStream() consistently returns an empty array despite successful registration and Bluetooth HFP audio connection.
Environment

Meta AI App: v251.0.0.58.166
Glasses Firmware: v21.0 (Wayfarer model)
Developer Mode: Enabled (toggle visible in Meta AI > App Info)
iOS Version: 17.x
DAT SDK: v0.3.0

Issue Description
After successful registration (receiving authorityKey and constellationGroupId via callback), devicesStream() returns 0 devices. However, the glasses ARE connected via Bluetooth HFP - we can detect them through AVAudioSession and audio routes correctly to/from the glasses.
Logs

[MetaGlasses] Registration state changed: RegistrationState(rawValue: 3)
[MetaGlasses] → State: registered ✅
[MetaGlasses] Device stream update: 0 device(s) found
[MetaGlasses] No glasses connected via Bluetooth

// But HFP detection works:
[MetaGlasses] Available audio inputs:
[MetaGlasses]   - Mr Robot Glasses (BluetoothHFP)
[MetaGlasses] ✓ Glasses detected via Bluetooth HFP

// StreamSession stuck waiting:
[MetaGlasses] StreamSession state: waitingForDevice
[MetaGlasses] Active device changed: disconnected
What We've Verified
✅ Firmware v21.0 (above required v20)
✅ Developer mode enabled in Meta AI app
✅ Registration completes successfully with valid authorityKey
✅ Glasses paired and connected in iOS Bluetooth settings
✅ HFP audio working (can hear/speak through glasses)
✅ AutoDeviceSelector and single StreamSession instance pattern (per SDK docs)

Expected vs Actual Behavior
Expected: After registration, devicesStream() should emit the connected glasses device, allowing StreamSession to transition to .streaming state. Actual: devicesStream() stays empty, activeDeviceStream() emits nil, and StreamSession remains in .waitingForDevice indefinitely.

Questions
Is there an additional permission or configuration step required for devicesStream() to detect devices?
We noticed in Meta View > Connected Apps, our app shows a "no permissions" icon (unlike Garmin which shows full options). How do we trigger the camera permission dialog?

Is there a known issue with certain glasses models (Wayfarer) or firmware versions?
Additional Context

We've reviewed TurboMeta's implementation which uses similar patterns
This appears similar to issue #44 where metaAINotInstalled errors occur despite correct setup
Our Info.plist configuration matches the SDK documentation

Any guidance would be greatly appreciated. Happy to provide additional logs or test specific scenarios. Thanks, Anthony Chavez VoiceSwap Developer https://voiceswap.cc/

Is required to give the app permissions to use that feature on devices.

I'm Anthony Chavez, developer of VoiceSwap - a voice-activated crypto payments app for Meta Ray-Ban glasses. We're experiencing an issue where devicesStream() consistently returns an empty array despite successful registration and Bluetooth HFP audio connection.
Environment

Meta AI App: v251.0.0.58.166
Glasses Firmware: v21.0 (Wayfarer model)
Developer Mode: Enabled (toggle visible in Meta AI > App Info)
iOS Version: 17.x
DAT SDK: v0.3.0

Issue Description
After successful registration (receiving authorityKey and constellationGroupId via callback), devicesStream() returns 0 devices. However, the glasses ARE connected via Bluetooth HFP - we can detect them through AVAudioSession and audio routes correctly to/from the glasses.
Logs

[MetaGlasses] Registration state changed: RegistrationState(rawValue: 3)

title: "Swift task continuation misuse"
body:
Hi! Our app is mostly working, but we sometimes run into an issue where the stream refuses to start resulting in a "stuck" state. This happens when we stress test our connect/disconnect flow by taking off the glasses and putting them back on, or resuming the game after several hours (or other situations, we don't have a perfect repro case). When the issue occurs, we see an error pop up in the XCode debugger that says - 

"SWIFT TASK CONTINUATION MISUSE: stop() leaked its continuation without resuming it. This may cause tasks waiting on it to remain suspended forever."

As far as I can tell, this is what happened immediately prior to this issue occurring - 

- awaited streamSession.start()
- new stream status event: waiting
- new stream status event: streaming
- streamSession.start() awaiter completes
- after a few moments, take glasses off
- new stream status event: waiting
- SWIFT TASK CONTINUATION MISUSE: stop() leaked its continuation (etc.) 

Once this occurs, subsequent attempts to start a stream are not effective.

We were using the 0.2.0 framework, saw there was a potential fix in 0.2.1, and upgraded to 0.3.0, but we are still seeing the issue.

comments.author: metadavithom
comments.body:
Hi @jkant,

Thanks for reporting.

We have made improvements to the thread handling for v0.4 for swift, there were some priority related issues. Once that releases, It would be good to see if those have resolved this issue for you.

Thanks,

David.

We mostly see it when stress testing the app (for example, opening and closing the hinges repeatedly before mounting the display) and we are able to recover from the error after force closing our app. Just figured I would provide an update. I am enclosing logs from right around where the issue popped up.

title: [Out of Memory] Silent failures during streaming and capture
body:
When the glasses run out of available memory, all active streaming and capture flows start failing silently. No explicit error is returned, no failure callbacks are triggered, and the SDK does not surface any recoverable or terminal error state.

From the application side, operations appear to be running normally, but:
	•	video / audio streams stop producing data
	•	capture requests do not complete
	•	no reasonable error or warning is emitted to indicate an OOM condition

This makes the issue extremely hard to detect and debug, as the system enters a broken state without any signal to the developer.

The example application in this repository can also reproduce this behavior under memory pressure, which suggests this is a systemic SDK-level issue rather than an app-specific bug.

Expected behavior
	•	Explicit error or callback when memory limits are reached
	•	Clear indication that streaming/capture failed due to OOM
	•	Ability to gracefully stop, recover, or restart affected flows

Actual behavior
	•	Silent failure with no error reporting
	•	SDK remains in an unrecoverable state until restart

title: Privacy Manifest and Signature
body:
Hello,

I was trying to put out a build of my app on testflight with DAT, but the binary is invalid. Here is the exact email I got, but the gist is the sdk needs a privacy manifest

ITMS-91061: Missing privacy manifest - Your app includes “Frameworks/MWDATMockDevice.framework/MWDATMockDevice”, which includes nanopb, an SDK that was identified in the documentation as a commonly used third-party SDK. If a new app includes a commonly used third-party SDK, or an app update adds a new commonly used third-party SDK, the SDK must include a privacy manifest file. Please contact the provider of the SDK that includes this file to get an updated SDK version with a privacy manifest. For more details about this policy, including a list of SDKs that are required to include signatures and manifests, visit: https://developer.apple.com/support/third-party-SDK-requirements.

ITMS-91065: Missing signature - Your app includes “Frameworks/MWDATMockDevice.framework/MWDATMockDevice”, which includes nanopb, an SDK that was identified in the documentation as a commonly used third-party SDK. If a new app includes a commonly used third-party SDK, or an app update adds a new commonly used third-party SDK, the SDK must include a signature file. Please contact the provider of the SDK that includes this file to get an updated SDK version with a signature. For details about verifying the code signature for a third-party SDK, visit: https://developer.apple.com/documentation/xcode/verifying-the-origin-of-your-xcframeworks.

title: App Store Rejection Due to ExternalAccessory / MFi Requirement
body:
Hello,

We are currently using this library to integrate Meta Smart Glass functionality into our iOS application.

However, our app was rejected by Apple App Store Review because the binary appears to include MFi accessory support. Apple specifically mentioned that the app contains references to:

ExternalAccessory framework

UIBackgroundModes with external-accessory

Bluetooth accessory related capabilities

Apple requires MFi authorization (PPID) when these features are present, which is not feasible for third-party developers in our case.

We would like to ask:

Does this library internally use the ExternalAccessory framework?

Is there a way to disable accessory-level communication while still allowing media capture or SDK-based interaction?

Is there a recommended configuration for App Store compliant usage without triggering MFi requirements?

Our goal is to use Meta SDK functionality only for accessing media generated by Meta Smart Glass, without direct hardware communication.

Any guidance or configuration options would be greatly appreciated.

Thank you.

title: MetaAppID `"0"` causes `configurationInvalid` in Developer Mode
body:
The [docs](https://wearables.developer.meta.com/docs/build-integration-ios) say to set `MetaAppID: "0"` for Developer Mode, but `startRegistration()` throws `RegistrationError.configurationInvalid` when you do.

Setting `MetaAppID` to an empty string `""` works fine. The [CameraAccess sample](https://github.com/facebook/meta-wearables-dat-ios/blob/main/samples/CameraAccess/CameraAccess/Info.plist) actually does this too, it uses `$(META_APP_ID)` which is undefined in the build settings, so it resolves to `""`.

## Repro

1. Set `MetaAppID` = `"0"` in Info.plist (per the docs)
2. Enable Developer Mode in Meta AI app
3. `Wearables.configure()` succeeds
4. `Wearables.shared.startRegistration()` throws `configurationInvalid`

Changing `"0"` to `""` fixes it.

| MetaAppID | ClientToken | Result |
|-----------|-------------|--------|
| `""` | `""` | Works |
| `"0"` | `""` | `configurationInvalid` |
| Real ID | Real token | Works |

## Environment

- Meta Wearables DAT iOS 0.4.0
- iPhone 16 Pro, iOS 18
- Developer Mode enabled in Meta AI

## Reproduction repo

https://github.com/fabriborgobello/mwdat-bug-test

title: [Audio Quality] HFP microphone captures wearer's voice clearly but distant voices are barely audible
body:
## Problem Description                                                                                        
                                                                                                                
  When using the Meta Wearables DAT SDK to record audio via HFP (Hands-Free Profile) from Meta Ray-Ban glasses, 
  I'm experiencing a significant audio imbalance:                                                               
                                                                                                                
  - **The person wearing the glasses** is captured very clearly                                                 
  - **People in front of/facing the wearer** are barely audible or almost inaudible                             
                                                                                                                
  ## Environment                                                                                                
                                                                                                                
  - Device: Meta Ray-Ban (Gen 2)                                                                                
  - Platform: iOS                                                                                               
  - SDK Version: 0.4                                                                       
  - Audio Configuration: HFP with `.allowBluetooth` option                                                      
                                                                                                                
  ## Current Implementation                                                                                     
                                                                                                                
  ```swift                                                                                                      
  let audioSession = AVAudioSession.sharedInstance()                                                            
  try audioSession.setCategory(.playAndRecord, mode: .default, options: [.allowBluetooth])                      
  try audioSession.setActive(true, options: .notifyOthersOnDeactivation)
  ```                                        
                                                                                                                
  Recording at hardware sample rate (typically 8kHz via HFP).                                                   
                                                                                                                
  Questions                                                                                                     
                                                                                                                
  1. Is this expected behavior due to microphone placement/beamforming on the glasses?                          
  2. Are there any SDK settings or audio configurations that can improve pickup of voices facing the wearer?    
  3. Has anyone found a workaround to balance audio levels between the wearer and other speakers?

comments.author: cassiomachado
comments.body:
Hi @alessiobelli6-max  thank you for raising this. I will convert to a Discussion because this expected behavior due to microphone placement/beamforming, to give more people the chance to see/participate, and to provide more complete context.

That said, we are actively working on SDK enhancements for this in the near future.

We appreciate the feedback.

title: startRegistration() fails silently when Developer Mode is not enabled — no error, no log, no callback
body:
## Summary

When `startRegistration()` is called without Developer Mode enabled in the Meta AI app, the SDK fails **completely silently**:

- Registration state transitions from `.available` → `.registering` → back to `.available`
- - **No error is thrown** (the `try await` returns without error)
- - - **No `RegistrationError` is raised**
- - - - **No console log or warning** from the SDK
- - - - - The Meta AI app **never opens** for OAuth
This makes debugging extremely difficult. We spent hours investigating WebSocket issues, Info.plist configuration, portal settings, and URL handler code before discovering the root cause was simply that Developer Mode wasn't enabled.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Install the Meta AI app on iPhone (v254+)
2. 2. Do **NOT** enable Developer Mode (Settings → App Info → tap version 5x)
3. 3. Configure a new iOS app with MWDAT SDK v0.4.0
4. 4. Set `MetaAppID` to empty string in Info.plist (Developer Mode config)
5. 5. Call `try await Wearables.shared.startRegistration()`
## Expected Behavior

The SDK should provide **clear feedback** when registration cannot proceed. For example:

- Throw a `RegistrationError` with a message like: *"Developer Mode is not enabled in the Meta AI app. Enable it in Settings → App Info → tap App version 5 times."*
- - Or at minimum, log a warning to the console explaining why registration failed
## Actual Behavior

- `startRegistration()` returns without error
- - Registration state briefly goes to `.registering` then silently reverts to `.available`
- - - No error, no log, no indication of what went wrong
- - - - The Meta AI app never opens — the OAuth flow never starts
## Environment

- MWDAT iOS SDK: 0.4.0
- - Meta AI app: v254+
- - - iOS: 18
- - - - Xcode: 16
## Suggestion

A descriptive error message here would save developers significant debugging time. The SDK clearly knows whether Developer Mode is active — surfacing that as a thrown error or at least a console warning would be a huge improvement.

Similarly, it would help to have better error messages for other silent failure modes we encountered:
- **Wrong/empty Bundle ID** in the Wearables Developer Center portal → silent fail
- - **Wrong/empty Team ID** in the portal → silent fail
- - - **MetaAppID set to actual app ID instead of empty string** while in Developer Mode → silent fail
All of these cause the same symptom (registering → available, no error) and could benefit from explicit error messages.

title: capturePhoto() limited to 1080x1440 (~1.5MP) — please support full 12MP sensor resolution
body:
The StreamSession.capturePhoto(format:) API returns images at only 1080×1440 (~1.5 megapixels), despite the Meta Ray-Ban glasses having a 12MP camera sensor. The PhotoCaptureFormat enum only offers .jpeg and .heic — there is no way to request a higher resolution capture.

This is consistent across multiple capture attempts and does not vary with StreamSessionConfig resolution settings (.high, .medium, .low only affect the video stream, not capturePhoto()).

Expected Behavior
capturePhoto() should be able to capture at the full sensor resolution (12MP / ~3000×4000). Ideally, PhotoCaptureFormat or a new PhotoCaptureConfig would allow specifying the desired resolution.


Environment

- MWDATCamera SDK: v0.4.0 (via SPM from meta-wearables-dat-ios)

- Glasses: Ray-Ban Meta (RBMeta09BZ)

- iOS: 26.x

- Device: iPhone (arm64)

title: Request for Additional Streaming Format Support (H264) or Conversion Guidance
body:
Hi team 👋

I’m currently integrating the Meta AI Wearables streaming API into an iOS application with the goal of streaming the camera feed to social media platforms (e.g., Instagram Live or other RTMP-based platforms).

After reviewing the documentation and experimenting with the SDK, I noticed that the streaming API currently provides two codec options through the VideoCodec enum:
	•	raw – Raw decompressed frames delivered as 420v YUV pixel buffers
	•	hvc1 – Compressed HEVC frames delivered as CMSampleBuffers

While these formats are useful for local processing, they are difficult to integrate with most social media streaming pipelines.

Most social platforms that accept external video streams (including Instagram Live Producer and many others) require an RTMP stream encoded with H264 (AVC) and AAC audio.

Because of this, the current formats create significant challenges:
	•	Using raw frames requires implementing a full real-time encoding pipeline (YUV → H264) on the client side.
	•	Using hvc1 frames requires decoding HEVC and re-encoding to H264, which introduces additional latency and processing overhead and is difficult to implement efficiently on iOS.

I spent about two weeks trying to build a stable conversion pipeline on iOS, but I was unable to find a reliable way to convert the current stream formats into an RTMP-compatible stream suitable for social media broadcasting.

Request

It would significantly simplify integration with external streaming platforms if one of the following improvements could be considered:
	1.	Support for H264 output in the streaming API (e.g., VideoCodec.h264).
	2.	Delivery of H264 NAL units or an Annex-B formatted stream.
	3.	Official examples demonstrating how to convert the existing raw or hvc1 outputs into an RTMP stream on iOS.
	4.	Documentation describing the recommended pipeline for broadcasting the wearable camera feed to social media platforms.

Question

If anyone has already implemented a working pipeline that converts the current raw or hvc1 outputs into a compatible RTMP stream, I would greatly appreciate any guidance or examples.

Thank you for your help.

title: Request support for glasses-side preview/viewfinder during DAT app-controlled capture
body:
## Summary

  We are building an iOS app with the Meta Wearables DAT SDK for hands-free item capture. We can stream camera frames to the phone and trigger photo capture from the app, but we do not see a way to access or integrate the glasses-side preview/viewfinder that exists in the native wristband capture flow.

  ## What works today

  Using the public DAT iOS SDK, we are able to:

  - connect to the glasses
  - stream the camera feed to the phone
  - display a live preview on the phone
  - trigger app-controlled photo capture
  - receive the captured image in the app and upload it to our backend

  ## What seems to be missing

  We do not see a public way to:

  - show or control the glasses-side preview/viewfinder during app-controlled capture
  - invoke the native preview-and-capture workflow from DAT
  - give users an in-glasses framing experience while still using our own app capture pipeline

  ## Why this matters

  Our use case is hands-free item capture for e-commerce listing workflows.

  The ideal user flow is:

  1. user looks at an item
  2. user centers the item in the glasses preview
  3. user triggers capture hands-free (voice or wrist gesture)
  4. image goes directly into our app/backend workflow

  Phone-side preview is technically useful, but glasses-side framing is much more important for real-world use. Users need to be able to center the item they are photographing without relying on looking back at the phone.

  ## Request

  We would like one of the following:

  - support for accessing the glasses-side preview/viewfinder during DAT app-controlled capture
  - an API that allows apps to invoke or integrate with the native preview-and-capture workflow
  - confirmation that this is not currently supported in the public DAT SDK

  ## Additional context

  We have already built a working prototype around DAT streaming + app-controlled capture on iOS. The main blocker to product rollout is the lack of access to the in-glasses framing experience that users already get in the native capture flow.

  If helpful, we can share more detail about our workflow and prototype.

  - give users an in-glasses framing experience while still using our own app capture pipeline

  ## Why this matters

  Our use case is hands-free item capture for e-commerce listing workflows.

  The ideal user flow is:

  1. user looks at an item
  2. user centers the item in the glasses preview
  3. user triggers capture hands-free (voice or wrist gesture)
  4. image goes directly into our app/backend workflow

  Phone-side preview is technically useful, but glasses-side framing is much more important for real-world use. Users need to be able to center the item they are photographing without relying on looking back at the phone.

  ## Request

  We would like one of the following:

  - support for accessing the glasses-side preview/viewfinder during DAT app-controlled capture
  - an API that allows apps to invoke or integrate with the native preview-and-capture workflow
  - confirmation that this is not currently supported in the public DAT SDK

  ## Additional context

  We have already built a working prototype around DAT streaming + app-controlled capture on iOS. The main blocker to product rollout is the lack of access to the in-glasses framing experience that users already get in the native capture flow.

  If helpful, we can share more detail about our workflow and prototype.

title: Internal error on registration — Application ID integration, Gen 2 Ray-Ban Meta
body:
## Summary

Getting **"Internal error — The operation could not be completed"** when tapping "Connect my glasses" and being redirected to the Meta AI app for registration.

## Setup

- **Glasses**: Ray-Ban Meta Gen 2, firmware up to date
- **iPhone**: 14 Pro Max, iOS 18.6.2
- **Meta AI app**: v264.1.0.17.167
- **DAT SDK**: 0.4.0 (via Swift Package Manager)
- **Xcode**: 26.3

## Configuration

Using **Application ID integration** (not Developer Mode — the toggle does not appear in Meta AI app despite tapping App Info version number 5 times, having a verified account on wearables.developer.meta.com with organization and project created).

**Info.plist MWDAT section:**
```xml
<key>MWDAT</key>
<dict>
    <key>AppLinkURLScheme</key>
    <string>cameraaccess://</string>
    <key>MetaAppID</key>
    <string>2347949502358249</string>
    <key>ClientToken</key>
    <string>AR|2347949502358249|ea44bf325492bc9145f2b078814d3c0c</string>
    <key>TeamID</key>
    <string>$(DEVELOPMENT_TEAM)</string>
</dict>
```

**Wearables Developer Center project settings:**
- Bundle ID: `com.nebg.truckcapture`
- Team ID: `69LJZ7262X`
- Universal link: `https://nebget.github.io/truck-diagnostics`
- AASA file verified accessible at that URL

## Behavior

1. Tap "Connect my glasses" in our app
2. Redirected to Meta AI app
3. Glasses visible in background
4. Popup: "Internal error — The operation could not be completed"
5. Only option is "OK", then stuck in Meta AI app

If I force close Meta AI and retry, I get "Device not connected" first, then "Internal error" on second attempt.

## Questions

1. Is there a propagation delay after creating a project on the wearables developer center?
2. Are the MetaAppID and ClientToken from the wearables developer center documentation page my actual credentials, or example values?
3. Is there anything else needed beyond the MWDAT Info.plist config + wearables developer center project setup?

## Environment

Similar to issue #18 — same error, same flow. That issue was resolved by enabling Developer Mode, but the Developer Mode toggle is not appearing for us.

title: How to get landscape video frames from StreamSession?
body:
Is there any way to receive camera video frames in **landscape** orientation from `StreamSession`?

All three `StreamingResolution` cases (`.low / .medium / .high`) return 9:16 portrait (`360×640 / 504×896 / 720×1280`), and I can't find any `orientation` / `aspectRatio` / `crop` option on `StreamSessionConfig` or `VideoFrame`. Verified on SDK **v0.5.0** and **v0.6.0**.

Is landscape supported anywhere, or is there a planned API for it?

Glasses: Ray-Ban Meta Gen 2.

title: Mock Device Kit does not register
body:
I'm trying the sample on a Samsung Galaxy S25 Ultra. Just adding a GitHub token then click build and play in Android Studio.

I've enabled Meta AI Developer mode. 

In the sample app, I click debug icon, click Pair Rayban Meta - a card appears. I swipe down and I don't see any glasses registered. If I click Start Streaming it asks for permissions. I confirm Always Allow but just see a white screen. I can stop streaming and try Power On, Unfold, Don but there is no feedback this worked. Start Streaming always does the same white screen.

I don't see anything like the screen shot here:
https://wearables.developer.meta.com/docs/mock-device-kit

No obvious errors in the log just:

Key com.meta.wearable.mwdat.APPLICATION_ID expected String but value was a java.lang.Integer.  The default value <null> was returned.
2025-12-06 12:55:01.155 14543-14543 Bundle                  com...ternalsampleapps.cameraaccess  W  Attempt to cast generated internal exception: (Ask Gemini)
                                                                                                    java.lang.ClassCastException: java.lang.Integer cannot be cast to java.lang.String
com.meta.wearable.mwdat.CLIENT_TOKEN not found in manifest metadata
2025-12-06 12:55:01.156 14543-14543 MetaWearablesConfig     com...ternalsampleapps.cameraaccess  W  com.meta.wearable.mwdat.ANALYTICS_OPT_OUT not found in manifest metadata

Also note if I select a Video from my phone it always reports:
2025-12-06 12:56:28.824 14543-14764 MediaStrea...ockService com...ternalsampleapps.cameraaccess  E  Failed to start mock streaming: Video frames do not match the requested video config

comments.author: metadavithom
comments.body:
Hi @rikturnbull, 

I think the issue will be this message in your output:

"Video frames do not match the requested video config"

In order to see something on the video stream when using a mock device, you have to provide a video and your video must be in h265 format, as we don't transcode for you. Try an h265 video and that should appear instead of a white background.
See more information here: https://wearables.developer.meta.com/docs/mock-device-kit#streaming-video

Thanks,

David.

comments.author: Akhil-Bhakar
comments.body:
Hi @rikturnbull,

I am also seeing a white screen once after clicking the 'Start Streaming' button. Could you let me know if you found a fix for this issue?

Thank you.

comments.author: rikturnbull
comments.body: Hi @Akhil-Bhakar - I didn't resolve for streaming and haven't tried the video fix yet. @metadavithom are we supposed to be able to live stream the camera using the mock device?

comments.author: metadavithom
comments.body:
Hi @Akhil-Bhakar and @rikturnbull - if you are using a Mock Device through the debug menu, you are simulating hardware. That doesn't have a real camera to stream, so you'll just get a white image by default. If you do want to use a Mock Device, you are doing the right steps by pairing a device, don-ing it, etc, but also need to first select a video for the Mock Device, it will then stream that for you and you should see it in the app, as long as the video is of the right format. For more information see the documentation here: [Simulating Media Streaming](https://wearables.developer.meta.com/docs/mock-device-kit#simulating-media-streaming)

If you want to stream from a physical pair of glasses, don't use the debug menu in the sample, just ensure they are paired in the MetaAI app and click Start Streaming.

title: “Something Went Wrong” Error When Starting Camera Streaming
body: Hi all, I am trying the camera sample application on my Android device. I installed the app on the device, tapped on the Debug icon, and selected Pair Ray-Ban Meta and swiped down the Mock Device Kit menu. Then I clicked Start Streaming. The camera permission prompt appeared and I selected Always Allow, but the app still doesn’t get camera access. Instead, it shows “Something went wrong.” I tried multiple times, including reopening the app, but the issue persists. May I know why it is not granting camera access?

comments.author: metadavithom
comments.body:
Hi @Akhil-Bhakar - are you wanting to use the sample app with a simulated device or real hardware?

The debug menu is for the Mock Device Kit functionality - you can see the documentation [here](https://wearables.developer.meta.com/docs/mock-device-kit) and a step-by-step to follow in order to setup the virtual glasses and provide a video to stream as if it was being seen by the glasses. Your description sounds like you are pairing glasses but not "turning them on" or "don-ing" them, so they aren't active.

If you are using real hardware, see the instructions [here](https://wearables.developer.meta.com/docs/getting-started-toolkit/#setting-up-your-glasses) for setting them up.

body:
# Android build fails with duplicate com.facebook.* classes when using mwdat-core in Expo / React Native

## Description

I’m trying to build an [Expo module](https://docs.expo.dev/modules/third-party-library/) to integrate **mwdat** into a React Native / Expo app.

- ✅ iOS build works correctly
- ❌ Android build fails during the build phase

---

## Environment

- Expo (React Native) project
- Android (standard Expo / React Native setup)
- `com.meta.wearable:mwdat-core:0.3.0`

---

## Build error

During the Android build, Gradle fails with duplicate class errors:

```
Execution failed for task ':app:checkDebugDuplicateClasses'.

Duplicate class com.facebook.common.logging.FLog found in modules
fbcore-3.6.0.aar (com.facebook.fresco:fbcore:3.6.0)
and
mwdat-core-0.3.0.aar (com.meta.wearable:mwdat-core:0.3.0)
```


Similar duplicate errors occur for many classes under:

- `com.facebook.common.*`
- `com.facebook.jni.*`
- `com.facebook.proguard.annotations.*`

(See full stack trace above.)

---

## Analysis

After investigation, it appears that **`mwdat-core-0.3.0.aar` bundles Facebook libraries directly inside its AAR**, including:

- Fresco (`com.facebook.common.*`)
- fbjni (`com.facebook.jni.*`)
- Facebook proguard annotations

These same libraries are already present in React Native / Expo, which causes **duplicate classes at build time**.

Because these classes are **physically embedded in the mwdat-core AAR (not declared as Gradle dependencies)**, standard Gradle solutions such as:

- `exclude`
- `resolutionStrategy.force`

do not resolve the issue.

---

## Expected behavior

Ideally, `mwdat-core` should:

- Not bundle Facebook / Fresco / fbjni classes directly, **or**
- Provide a variant without these dependencies, **or**
- Declare them as proper Gradle dependencies so they can be resolved normally

This would allow mwdat to be used in React Native / Expo Android projects without conflicts.

---

## Questions

- Is there a recommended Android configuration for React Native / Expo?
- Is there a variant of the SDK without bundled Facebook dependencies?
- Is a fix planned for this packaging issue?

Thanks in advance 🙏

RESOLUTION: ## Android – Fix build error by using local AAR dependencies

You can avoid this issue by downloading the required AARs via Gradle and adding them directly to your Expo module.

### 1. Add the AAR files

Download the `.aar` files and place them in the following directory of your Expo module:
android/libs/


Example:
- `mwdat-core-0.3.0.aar`
- `mwdat-camera-0.3.0.aar`
- `mwdat-mockdevice-0.3.0.aar`

---

### 2. Configure `expo-module.config.json`

Update your `expo-module.config.json` to declare the local AAR projects:

```json
{
  "android": {
    "gradleAarProjects": [
      {
        "name": "mwdat-core",
        "aarFilePath": "android/libs/mwdat-core-0.3.0.aar"
      },
      {
        "name": "mwdat-camera",
        "aarFilePath": "android/libs/mwdat-camera-0.3.0.aar"
      },
      {
        "name": "mwdat-mockdevice",
        "aarFilePath": "android/libs/mwdat-mockdevice-0.3.0.aar"
      }
    ],
    "modules": ["expo.modules.metawearables.ExpoMetaWearablesModule"]
  }
}
```

### 3. Update build.gradle

Add the following dependencies to your module’s build.gradle file:

```gradle
dependencies {
  implementation project(":${project.name}$mwdat-camera")
  implementation project(":${project.name}$mwdat-core")
  implementation project(":${project.name}$mwdat-mockdevice")
}
```

title: Unable to connect Meta Ray-Ban Gen 2 glasses – redirected to Meta AI app but nothing happens
body:
## Description
When trying to connect my Meta Ray-Ban Gen 2 glasses using the example app, I am redirected to the Meta AI app.  
However, after clicking **"Connect"**, nothing happens and the connection never completes.

I observe the same behavior when using my own custom app.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Open the example app
2. Start the glasses connection flow
3. Get redirected to the Meta AI app
4. Tap **"Connect"**
5. Nothing happens (no error, no connection)

## Expected Behavior
The glasses should successfully connect to the app after confirming the connection in the Meta AI app.

## Actual Behavior
- The Meta AI app opens correctly
- Tapping **"Connect"** does nothing
- No error message or feedback is shown
- The app never receives a connection result

## Devices & Environment
- **Glasses:** Meta Ray-Ban Gen 2
- **Android devices tested:**
  - POCO phone – Android 14
  - Pixel 9 – Android 16
- **Meta AI app:** latest version from Play Store
- **Glasses status:** factory reset performed before testing

## Additional Notes
- Issue occurs consistently on both devices
- Factory reset of the glasses did not resolve the problem
- Happens with both the example app and a custom implementation

title: Meta Glasses SDK video stream FPS drops dramatically when any video/audio encoder is active
body:
## Environment

- **Device**: Pixel 9a
- **Android version**: 16
- **Meta Glasses SDK version**: 0.3.0
- **Meta Glasses model**: Ray-Ban Meta Smart Glasses

## Description

The Meta SDK video stream experiences severe FPS drops (from ~24 fps to ~1-3 fps) whenever **any video or audio encoder is active on the device**, regardless of whether it's hardware or software encoding, and even when the encoder processes completely different video sources (not Meta frames).

This makes it impossible to implement real-time streaming applications (RTMP, WebRTC, etc.) using Meta Glasses video while encoding the stream.

## Expected Behavior

The Meta SDK should deliver video frames at a consistent ~24 fps, even when other video/audio encoders are active on the device, as long as system resources are available.

## Actual Behavior

As soon as **any** `MediaCodec` video encoder is initialized and started (even before processing any frames), the Meta SDK video stream slows down to ~1-3 fps.

## Steps to Reproduce

### Baseline (no encoder): ✅ Works normally

```kotlin
// Just collect Meta video frames without any processing
session.videoStream.collect { videoFrame ->
    Log.d("Meta", "Frame received")
}
```

**Result**: ✅ **5-24 FPS** (good performance)

```
📹 Frame received - FPS: 28.57 (interval: 35 ms)
📹 Frame received - FPS: 10.42 (interval: 96 ms)
📹 Frame received - FPS: 15.63 (interval: 64 ms)
⏱️ AVERAGE FPS: 12.01 fps
```

### With H264 hardware encoder: ❌ Broken

```kotlin
// Initialize a separate H264 hardware encoder (not processing Meta frames yet)
val encoder = MediaCodec.createEncoderByType(MediaFormat.MIMETYPE_VIDEO_AVC)
val format = MediaFormat.createVideoFormat(MediaFormat.MIMETYPE_VIDEO_AVC, 896, 504)
format.setInteger(MediaFormat.KEY_BIT_RATE, 2500000)
format.setInteger(MediaFormat.KEY_FRAME_RATE, 24)
format.setInteger(MediaFormat.KEY_I_FRAME_INTERVAL, 2)
format.setInteger(MediaFormat.KEY_COLOR_FORMAT, MediaCodecInfo.CodecCapabilities.COLOR_FormatSurface)

encoder.configure(format, null, null, MediaCodec.CONFIGURE_FLAG_ENCODE)
encoder.start()

// Collect Meta frames (encoder exists but not processing Meta frames)
session.videoStream.collect { videoFrame ->
    // Meta SDK throttles to ~1 fps
}
```

**Result**: ❌ **0.5-3 FPS** (completely broken)

```
📹 Frame received - FPS: 1.05 (interval: 953 ms)
📹 Frame received - FPS: 0.99 (interval: 1009 ms)
📹 Frame received - FPS: 1.52 (interval: 659 ms)
⏱️ AVERAGE FPS: 1.38 fps
```

### With H264 SOFTWARE encoder: ❌ Still broken

```kotlin
// Force software encoder (no hardware conflict possible)
val softwareEncoders = CodecUtil.getAllSoftwareEncoders(CodecUtil.H264_MIME, true)
val encoder = MediaCodec.createByCodecName(softwareEncoders[0].name)

// Same configuration as above...
encoder.configure(format, null, null, MediaCodec.CONFIGURE_FLAG_ENCODE)
encoder.start()

session.videoStream.collect { videoFrame ->
    // Meta SDK still throttles to ~1 fps
}
```

**Result**: ❌ **1-3 FPS** (still broken, even with software encoder)

```
📹 Frame received - FPS: 1.61 (interval: 622 ms)
📹 Frame received - FPS: 0.98 (interval: 1022 ms)
📹 Frame received - FPS: 2.48 (interval: 403 ms)
⏱️ AVERAGE FPS: 1.70 fps
```

## Analysis

### What we tested

1. ✅ **Hardware H264 encoder** → Meta throttles to ~1 fps
2. ✅ **Software H264 encoder** → Meta throttles to ~1 fps
3. ✅ **Audio AAC encoder** → Meta throttles to ~1 fps
4. ✅ **Multiple RTMP libraries** (HaishinKit, RootEncoder) → Same issue with both
5. ✅ **Separate threads with different priorities** → No improvement
6. ✅ **Reduced encoder parameters** (480p @ 10fps, 500kbps) → No improvement

### What doesn't cause the issue

- ❌ Hardware encoder resource contention (software encoder has same issue)
- ❌ CPU load (encoders run on separate low-priority threads)
- ❌ Specific RTMP library implementation (happens with all libraries)
- ❌ Frame processing time (Meta frames process in < 5ms)

### Hypothesis

The Meta SDK appears to **detect system encoder activity** (via some monitoring mechanism) and **automatically throttles its video stream FPS** as a "performance optimization" or "resource management" strategy.

This behavior occurs **before** any actual resource conflict, suggesting it's **preventive throttling** based on detecting encoder initialization, not actual system load.

## Impact

This issue makes it **impossible** to build real-time streaming applications with Meta Glasses on Android, such as:

- 📹 Live streaming to RTMP/RTSP servers (Twitch, YouTube, etc.)
- 🎥 Video recording with encoding
- 🌐 WebRTC video calls
- 📡 Real-time video transmission

All these use cases require video encoding while capturing Meta Glasses frames.

## Questions

1. Is there a **configuration option** to disable this automatic FPS throttling?
2. Is there a **high-performance mode** or **priority flag** for the video stream?
3. Is this behavior **intentional** or a bug?
4. Are there **recommended patterns** for encoding Meta video streams in real-time?

## Workaround Attempts

We tried:

- ✅ Using separate threads with `THREAD_PRIORITY_URGENT_DISPLAY`
- ✅ Software encoding instead of hardware
- ✅ Reduced encoder parameters
- ✅ Different RTMP/encoding libraries

**None of these worked.**

## Request

Could the Meta SDK team please:

1. Investigate why encoder activity causes FPS throttling
2. Provide a configuration option to disable this throttling for high-performance use cases
3. Document the expected behavior when using encoders alongside Meta video capture
4. Consider that developers need to encode video streams in real-time for legitimate use cases

## Code Sample

Minimal reproduction case:

```kotlin
// Initialize Meta session
val session = Wearables.streamManager.startStreamSession(context)

// Start video stream collection
session.videoStream.collect { videoFrame ->
    val fps = calculateFPS() // Measures ~20+ fps initially
    Log.d("Meta", "FPS: $fps")
}

// Now initialize any encoder (doesn't even need to process frames)
val encoder = MediaCodec.createEncoderByType(MediaFormat.MIMETYPE_VIDEO_AVC)
val format = MediaFormat.createVideoFormat(MediaFormat.MIMETYPE_VIDEO_AVC, 640, 480)
format.setInteger(MediaFormat.KEY_BIT_RATE, 1000000)
format.setInteger(MediaFormat.KEY_FRAME_RATE, 24)
encoder.configure(format, null, null, MediaCodec.CONFIGURE_FLAG_ENCODE)
encoder.start()

// FPS immediately drops to ~1-3 fps
```

## Additional Context

This issue has been verified across:

- Multiple devices
- Multiple encoding libraries (HaishinKit, RootEncoder)
- Both hardware and software encoders
- Both video and audio encoders

The consistent behavior across all these scenarios strongly suggests this is a Meta SDK limitation rather than an implementation issue.

Thank you for investigating this issue!

comments.author: QuentinGrange
comments.body:
I finally managed to build something stable.

I implemented my own audio and video encoders to build an RTMP stream using RootEncoder and RTMPClient. I did not use RootEncoder’s built-in VideoEncoder and AudioEncoder.

Using MediaMixer from HaishinKit was even worse — I was dropping below 1 FPS.

I also had to upscale the frames because after 10–30 seconds the video resolution was automatically decreasing.

On iOS, however, I was able to build an RTMP stream in about 20 lines of code using HaishinKit, without any FPS drop at all.

I really hope Meta will eventually provide a better SDK (similar to Mentra: https://docs.mentraglass.com/app-devs/getting-started/overview), which is far easier to use.

comments.author: oytunakman
comments.body: Just tested this issue extensively and can confirm the throttling happens even without MediaCodec encoders. Simply calling LiveKit.create(context) drops glasses camera from 24 FPS to 1-3 FPS instantly. The Meta SDK seems to detect WebRTC components (PeerConnectionFactory, etc.) and throttles preemptively. Without LiveKit: perfect 24 FPS. With LiveKit: throttled to 1-3 FPS, even with no tracks, no data sending, nothing. This makes real-time streaming apps basically impossible. Would be great if the throttling could be relaxed or at least documented clearly so devs know upfront.

comments.author: QuentinGrange
comments.body: Yes, this matches my findings as well. The FPS drop is triggered simply by initializing a WebRTC / streaming stack, not by actual media encoding or transmission.

embed_text:
TITLE: Meta Glasses SDK video stream FPS drops dramatically when any video/audio encoder is active
PROBLEM: ## Environment

- **Device**: Pixel 9a
- **Android version**: 16
- **Meta Glasses SDK version**: 0.3.0
- **Meta Glasses model**: Ray-Ban Meta Smart Glasses

## Description

The Meta SDK video stream experiences severe FPS drops (from ~24 fps to ~1-3 fps) whenever **any video or audio encoder is active on the device**, regardless of whether it's hardware or software encoding, and even when the encoder processes completely different video sources (not Meta frames).

This makes it impossible to implement real-time streaming applications (RTMP, WebRTC, etc.) using Meta Glasses video while encoding the stream.

## Expected Behavior

The Meta SDK should deliver video frames at a consistent ~24 fps, even when other video/audio encoders are active on the device, as long as system resources are available.

## Actual Behavior

As soon as **any** `MediaCodec` video encoder is initialized and started (even before processing any frames), the Meta SDK video stream slows down to ~1-3 fps.

## Steps to Reproduce

### Baseline (no encoder): ✅ Works normally

```kotlin
// Just collect Meta video frames without any processing
session.videoStream.collect { videoFrame ->
    Log.d("Meta", "Frame received")
}
```

**Result**: ✅ **5-24 FPS** (good performance)

```
📹 Frame received - FPS: 28.57 (interval: 35 ms)
📹 Frame received - FPS: 10.42 (interval: 96 ms)
📹 Frame received - FPS: 15.63 (interval: 64 ms)
⏱️ AVERAGE FPS: 12.01 fps
```

### With H264 hardware encoder: ❌ Broken

```kotlin
// Initialize a separate H264 hardware encoder (not processing Meta frames yet)
val encoder = MediaCodec.createEncoderByType(MediaFormat.MIMETYPE_VIDEO_AVC)
val format = MediaFormat.createVideoFormat(MediaFormat.MIMETYPE_VIDEO_AVC, 896, 504)
format.setInteger(MediaFormat.KEY_BIT_RATE, 2500000)
format.setInteger(MediaFormat.KEY_FRAME_RATE, 24)
format.setInteger(MediaFormat.KEY_I_FRAME_INTERVAL, 2)
format.setInteger(MediaFormat.KEY_COLOR_FORMAT, MediaCodecInfo.CodecCapabilities.COLOR_FormatSurface)

encoder.configure(format, null, null, MediaCodec.CONFIGURE_FLAG_ENCODE)
encoder.start()

// Collect Meta frames (encoder exists but not processing Meta frames)
session.videoStream.collect { videoFrame ->
    // Meta SDK throttles to ~1 fps
}
```

**Result**: ❌ **0.5-3 FPS** (completely broken)

```
📹 Frame received - FPS: 1.05 (interval: 953 ms)
📹 Frame received - FPS: 0.99 (interval: 1009 ms)
📹 Frame received - FPS: 1.52 (interval: 659 ms)
⏱️ AVERAGE FPS: 1.38 fps
```

### With H264 SOFTWARE encoder: ❌ Still broken

```kotlin
// Force software encoder (no hardware conflict possible)
val softwareEncoders = CodecUtil.getAllSoftwareEncoders(CodecUtil.H264_MIME, true)
val encoder = MediaCodec.createByCodecName(softwareEncoders[0].name)

// Same configuration as above...
encoder.configure(format, null, null, MediaCodec.CONFIGURE_FLAG_ENCODE)
encoder.start()

session.videoStream.collect { videoFrame ->
    // Meta SDK still throttles to ~1 fps
}
```

**Result**: ❌ **1-3 FPS** (still broken, even with software encoder)

```
📹 Frame received - FPS: 1.61 (interval: 622 ms)
📹 Frame received - FPS: 0.98 (interval: 1022 ms)
📹 Frame received - FPS: 2.48 (interval: 403 ms)
⏱️ AVERAGE FPS: 1.70 fps
```

## Analysis

### What we tested

1. ✅ **Hardware H264 encoder** → Meta throttles to ~1 fps
2. ✅ **Software H264 encoder** → Meta throttles to ~1 fps
3. ✅ **Audio AAC encoder** → Meta throttles to ~1 fps
4. ✅ **Multiple RTMP libraries** (HaishinKit, RootEncoder) → Same issue with both
5. ✅ **Separate threads with different priorities** → No improvement
6. ✅ **Reduced encoder parameters** (480p @ 10fps, 500kbps) → No improvement

### What doesn't cause the issue

- ❌ Hardware encoder resource contention (software encoder has same issue)
- ❌ CPU load (encoders run on separate low-priority threads)
- ❌ Specific RTMP library implementation (happens with all libraries)
- ❌ Frame processing time (Meta frames process in 
    val fps = calculateFPS() // Measures ~20+ fps initially
    Log.d("Meta", "FPS: $fps")
}

// Now initialize any encoder (doesn't even need to process frames)
val encoder = MediaCodec.createEncoderByType(MediaFormat.MIMETYPE_VIDEO_AVC)
val format = MediaFormat.createVideoFormat(MediaFormat.MIMETYPE_VIDEO_AVC, 640, 480)
format.setInteger(MediaFormat.KEY_BIT_RATE, 1000000)
format.setInteger(MediaFormat.KEY_FRAME_RATE, 24)
encoder.configure(format, null, null, MediaCodec.CONFIGURE_FLAG_ENCODE)
encoder.start()

// FPS immediately drops to ~1-3 fps
```

## Additional Context

This issue has been verified across:

- Multiple devices
- Multiple encoding libraries (HaishinKit, RootEncoder)
- Both hardware and software encoders
- Both video and audio encoders

The consistent behavior across all these scenarios strongly suggests this is a Meta SDK limitation rather than an implementation issue.

Thank you for investigating this issue!

title: AARs missing consumer proguard rules (R8 strips JNI-loaded classes)
body:
```
ERROR: R8: Missing class com.facebook.common.preconditions.Preconditions                                                                                                                       
  (referenced from: void com.facebook.debug.log.BLog.setLogLevel(int))    
Missing class com.facebook.infer.annotation.Nullsafe                                                                                                                                           
  (referenced from: com.facebook.common.collectlite.RingBuffer and 38 other contexts)                                                                                                          
Missing class com.facebook.infer.annotation.NullsafeStrict
  (referenced from: com.facebook.debug.log.BLog)
Missing class com.facebook.secure.sanitizer.intf.DataSanitizer
  (referenced from: com.facebook.secure.sanitizer.SensitiveDataSanitizer)
```

I had to add a ProGuard consumer rules file to work around this. Is this safe, or is this pointing to the 0.4.0 release having issues?

```
-dontwarn com.facebook.common.preconditions.Preconditions
-dontwarn com.facebook.infer.annotation.Nullsafe
-dontwarn com.facebook.infer.annotation.NullsafeStrict
-dontwarn com.facebook.secure.sanitizer.intf.DataSanitizer
```

If this is required and safe to do, these consumer rules should be included in the AARs.

comments.author: bddckr
comments.body:
The original `-dontwarn` classes from 0.4.0 are resolved in 0.5.0 — those symbols are now either bundled or the transitive dependencies changed.

However, the broader issue remains: the AARs still don't ship consumer proguard rules despite loading classes via JNI/SoLoader. With R8 enabled, classes like `com.facebook.wearable.airshield.stream.Framing` are stripped because R8 can't see the native references, causing `ClassNotFoundException` / `ExceptionInInitializerError` at runtime.

We're working around this with:

```
-keep class com.facebook.** { *; }
-keep class com.meta.** { *; }

-dontwarn com.facebook.common.build.BuildConstants
-dontwarn com.facebook.common.errorreporting.**
-dontwarn com.facebook.common.stringformat.StringFormatUtil
-dontwarn com.facebook.secure.urifilter.UriFilter
-dontwarn com.facebook.secure.uriparser.SecureUriParser
-dontwarn javax.inject.Provider
```

The `-keep` rules are broad because it's unclear exactly which classes are loaded from native code. Ideally the AARs would ship consumer rules with the necessary `-keep` entries for JNI-accessed classes, plus `-dontwarn` for their own unbundled internal dependencies.

title: Very laggy streaming for the first 5-10 seconds
body:
**Environment**
Samsung Galaxy S23
Meta Glasses SDK version - 0.4.0
Meta Glasses model: Ray-Ban Meta Smart Glasses
Our own integration and the demo CameraAccess app

**Actual Behavior**
Both in our integration and **in the sample CameraAccess** app there is a significant initial delay (5-10 seconds) between receiving first frames from the glasses camera and the preview becoming responsive (e.g. following the movement of my head). After that time, it looks like the streaming catches up and then the frame rate stabilises. Lowering the streaming quality or frame rate doesn't help much. I'm using the default settings:

`StreamConfiguration(videoQuality = VideoQuality.MEDIUM, 24)`

There is also a significant delay when we invoke `capturePhoto` at the beginning of streaming.

**Expected Behavior**
The streaming should deliver a stable frame rate from the beginning, without delay. It is crucial when the user's goal is to take a picture.

title: mwdat-core AAR manifest leaks app-level startup initializers
body:
## Summary

The `mwdat-core` AAR's manifest includes an `androidx.startup.InitializationProvider` with initializer entries that don't belong in a library:

```xml
<provider
    android:name="androidx.startup.InitializationProvider"
    android:authorities="${applicationId}.androidx-startup"
    android:exported="false">
    <meta-data
        android:name="androidx.emoji2.text.EmojiCompatInitializer"
        android:value="androidx.startup" />
    <meta-data
        android:name="androidx.lifecycle.ProcessLifecycleInitializer"
        android:value="androidx.startup" />
    <meta-data
        android:name="androidx.profileinstaller.ProfileInstallerInitializer"
        android:value="androidx.startup" />
</provider>
```

These are app-level initializers that appear to have leaked into the published AAR from the build environment. A library shouldn't be contributing these — they're concerns of the consuming application, not the SDK.

This causes a crash at app startup when `emoji2` isn't on the consumer's runtime classpath (which it won't be unless pulled in by another dependency):

```
java.lang.RuntimeException: Unable to get provider androidx.startup.InitializationProvider:
  androidx.startup.StartupException: java.lang.ClassNotFoundException:
    androidx.emoji2.text.EmojiCompatInitializer
```

The same risk applies to `ProfileInstallerInitializer`.

## Workaround

Consuming apps can remove the offending entries from the merged manifest:

```xml
<provider
    android:name="androidx.startup.InitializationProvider"
    android:authorities="${applicationId}.androidx-startup"
    android:exported="false"
    tools:node="merge">
    <meta-data
        android:name="androidx.emoji2.text.EmojiCompatInitializer"
        tools:node="remove" />
</provider>
```

## Affected version

`com.meta.wearable:mwdat-core:0.4.0`

comments.body:
Thanks for looking into this!

Looks like your message is missing the workaround, but I can confirm that the workaround in my original message is working well. Some other dependencies of my app require `ProcessLifecycleInitializer` and `ProfileInstallerInitializer` is needed these days as well. Therefore I'm only removing `EmojiCompatInitializer` — my app doesn't depend on `emoji2`, so App Startup crashes trying to instantiate the class since it's not on the runtime classpath.

That all said, the main issue here is that the library doesn't depend on these either — they're transitive dependencies that leaked into the AAR manifest during the SDK's build. The SDK shouldn't be contributing them.

comments.author: alexsinkmeta
comments.body:
Thanks for confirming — and sorry about my earlier comment missing the actual workaround.
To confirm, your workaround is to remove only the EmojiCompatInitializer entry from the merged manifest (since your app doesn’t include emoji2 on the runtime classpath), e.g.:

```
<provider
    android:name="androidx.startup.InitializationProvider"
    android:authorities="${applicationId}.androidx-startup"
    android:exported="false"
    tools:node="merge">
  <meta-data
      android:name="androidx.emoji2.text.EmojiCompatInitializer"
      tools:node="remove" />
</provider>
```

That makes sense given the crash you’re seeing: App Startup attempts to instantiate androidx.emoji2.text.EmojiCompatInitializer, but the class isn’t present unless emoji2 is pulled in.
More importantly: agreed on the root cause. mwdat-core shouldn’t be contributing any of these app-level Startup initializers (EmojiCompatInitializer, ProcessLifecycleInitializer, ProfileInstallerInitializer) via its published AAR manifest. Even if some apps want ProcessLifecycleInitializer / ProfileInstallerInitializer, that should be an application concern, not something implicitly added by the SDK.
I’m escalating this to the build/publishing owners so we can:
identify why these initializers are leaking into the AAR manifest, and
ship a fix so consumers don’t need manifest overrides.
If you can share a minimal repro (or confirm whether you’re using 0.4.0 vs 0.5.0 and whether the crash reproduces with only mwdat-core added), that’ll help us validate the fix quickly.

Thanks,
-Alex
