title: Numeric APPLICATION_ID manifest value silently ignored
body:
## Summary

When following the [official setup docs](https://wearables.developer.meta.com/docs/build-integration-android), the recommended developer mode manifest entry:

```xml
<meta-data
    android:name="com.meta.wearable.mwdat.APPLICATION_ID"
    android:value="0" />
```

is silently ignored by the SDK. Android's manifest parser infers `"0"` as an `Integer`, and `Bundle.getString()` returns `null` when it encounters a non-String type:

```
Bundle  W  Key com.meta.wearable.mwdat.APPLICATION_ID expected String but value was a java.lang.Integer.  The default value <null> was returned.
Bundle  W  Attempt to cast generated internal exception:
           java.lang.ClassCastException: java.lang.Integer cannot be cast to java.lang.String
               at android.os.BaseBundle.getString(BaseBundle.java:1416)
               at com.meta.wearable.dat.core.internal.configuration.DatConfiguration$Companion.getStringFromManifest(...)
```

This is a well-known Android behavior with `<meta-data>` values that look numeric. The same issue will affect production apps whose Application IDs from the Wearables Developer Center happen to be purely numeric.

## Workaround

Prefix the value with `\` to force string type:

```xml
<meta-data
    android:name="com.meta.wearable.mwdat.APPLICATION_ID"
    android:value="\0" />
```

## Suggested fix

The SDK should read the value using `Bundle.get()` and call `.toString()`, rather than `Bundle.getString()`. The docs should also be updated to use `\0` instead of `0`.

## Affected version

`com.meta.wearable:mwdat-core:0.4.0`

SDK picks it up cleanly. Thanks!

title: RegistrationState.Unavailable reported but Wearables.startRegistration succeeds
body:
## Summary

`RegistrationState.Unavailable` is emitted by `Wearables.registrationState`, but calling `Wearables.startRegistration()` from that state transitions through `Registering` → `Registered` successfully. We'd expect `Unavailable` to mean registration cannot proceed.

## Steps to reproduce

1. Observe `RegistrationState.Unavailable` emitted from `Wearables.registrationState`
2. Call `Wearables.startRegistration()` anyway
3. State transitions: `Unavailable` → `Registering` → `Registered`

## Expected behavior

If the SDK reports `Unavailable`, either `startRegistration()` should fail or the SDK should not report `Unavailable` in the first place.

## Actual behavior

Registration succeeds despite the `Unavailable` state, suggesting the state is inaccurate.

## Impact

We use `RegistrationState` to decide whether to prompt the user to register. When `Unavailable` is reported, we skip the registration prompt — which means users get stuck waiting even though registration would work fine.

## Environment

- MWDAT version: `0.5.0` (`mwdat-core`)

title: Wearables.checkPermissionStatus returns INTERNAL_ERROR after registering from Unavailable state
body:
## Summary

After successfully registering via `Wearables.startRegistration()` (from the `Unavailable` state — see #67), calling `Wearables.checkPermissionStatus(Permission.CAMERA)` fails with `PermissionError.INTERNAL_ERROR` and the description "An unexpected internal error occurred".

## Steps to reproduce

1. Observe `RegistrationState.Unavailable` from `Wearables.registrationState`
2. Call `Wearables.startRegistration()` — state transitions to `Registered`
3. Call `Wearables.checkPermissionStatus(Permission.CAMERA)`
4. Result: failure with `PermissionError.INTERNAL_ERROR`

## Expected behavior

`checkPermissionStatus` should return the current permission status (e.g., `Denied` or `Granted`) so we can prompt the user.

## Actual behavior

The check fails with an internal error, blocking the entire camera permission flow.

## Related

- #67 — `RegistrationState.Unavailable` reported but registration succeeds

## Environment

- MWDAT version: `0.5.0` (`mwdat-core`)

comments.author: bddckr
comments.body:
## Additional findings

### 1. `INTERNAL_ERROR` also occurs transiently during BLE disconnection

The error isn't limited to registering from `Unavailable`. We also see `checkPermissionStatus` return `PermissionError.INTERNAL_ERROR` immediately after the glasses are doffed (hinges closed), when the BLE socket closes:

```
DAT:CORE:Wearables: Failed to check permission status for device ..., error: Protocol error Error(0xc004: INTERNAL_ERROR)
```

In this case the error is transient — once BLE reconnects and a new stream session is started, `checkPermissionStatus` returns `Granted` again.

### 2. `Wearables.reset()` makes the problem permanent

Calling `Wearables.reset()` + `Wearables.initialize()` as a recovery strategy makes the `INTERNAL_ERROR` from `checkPermissionStatus` **permanent** — it never recovers, even after granting permission via the dialog. Only a full process restart clears it.

It appears that `reset()` does not fully clean up internal SDK state. After `reset()` + `initialize()`, the SDK operates on top of stale state from the previous initialization.

**Workaround**: Avoid calling `Wearables.reset()`. When `checkPermissionStatus` returns `INTERNAL_ERROR`, skip the permission dialog and attempt `startStreamSession` directly — it may succeed if permission was previously granted.

comments.author: alexsinkmeta
comments.body:
We've believe we've identified and fixed the root cause of this issue. We are working on a fix for an upcoming SDK release.

Regarding the reset() behavior making the error permanent -- this is addressed by the same potential fix. 

Workaround (until the fix ships):
Re-registering the device forces a fresh session and resolves the INTERNAL_ERROR. We know this is inconvenient but it's the most reliable workaround available.

Expected requirements to verify your setup(let me know if this is correct so we can be sure this addresses the issue):

SDK initialized with Wearables.initialize(context) before any API calls
Device registered and in range
Bluetooth enabled and BLUETOOTH_CONNECT permission granted
Internet connection available during registration

comments.author: bddckr
comments.body:
> Re-registering the device forces a fresh session and resolves the INTERNAL_ERROR. We know this is inconvenient but it's the most reliable workaround available.

I can confirm this workaround works 100% of the time.

> SDK initialized with Wearables.initialize(context) before any API calls
Device registered and in range
Bluetooth enabled and BLUETOOTH_CONNECT permission granted
Internet connection available during registration

Yes to all of these! ✅

---

Ever since I've removed the call to `reset()`, this issue is no longer appearing often. I only see it sporadically as part of doffing the glasses, as explained above.

Thanks for looking into this, much appreciated.

title: DAT 0.5.0: Camera permission "Always allow" grant not propagating — checkPermissionStatus stays Denied, StreamSession goes straight to STOPPED
body:
## Summary

After a user grants camera access via `Wearables.RequestPermissionContract` by selecting **"Always allow" → Confirm** in the Meta AI app dialog, `Wearables.checkPermissionStatus(Permission.CAMERA)` continues to return `PermissionStatus.Denied`, and any subsequent `Wearables.startStreamSession` transitions straight to `StreamSessionState.STOPPED` without ever reaching `STREAMING`.

This is on a developer-mode app registered via the Wearables Developer Center (Internal Testing release channel), built against DAT 0.5.0 on Ray-Ban Meta (Gen 2) glasses.

## Environment

- DAT SDK: `com.meta.wearable:mwdat-core:0.5.0`, `mwdat-camera:0.5.0`
- Glasses: Ray-Ban Meta (Gen 2) — `RB Meta 00DK`, firmware `12.14.7`
- Meta AI app (`com.facebook.stella`): `267.1.0.67.173`
- Phone: Google Pixel 9, Android 14 (minSdk 34, targetSdk 34)
- App status: registered via Wearables Developer Center, Internal Testing channel, developer mode ON in Meta AI. `APPLICATION_ID` + `CLIENT_TOKEN` set in manifest.

## Repro

1. Pair + register via `Wearables.startRegistration(activity)` → succeeds, state becomes `RegistrationState.Registered`.
2. Device appears in `Wearables.devices`; `devicesMetadata[id].name` is `"RB Meta 00DK"`.
3. Launch `Wearables.RequestPermissionContract()` for `Permission.CAMERA` from a `ComponentActivity`.
4. Meta AI shows the dialog:
   > Allow Crow's Nest to access the camera on your Meta devices?
   > Always allow / Allow once / Don't allow — Confirm
5. User picks **Always allow** and taps **Confirm**. Dialog dismisses.
6. Back in app, `Wearables.checkPermissionStatus(Permission.CAMERA)` still returns:
   ```
   DatResult(result=Success(Denied))
   ```
7. `Wearables.startStreamSession(ctx, AutoDeviceSelector(), StreamConfiguration(videoQuality=MEDIUM, frameRate=24))` emits a single state (`STOPPED`) and never transitions to `STARTING → STARTED → STREAMING`.

## Logcat (relevant lines)

```
D/ObjectAPI( 4136): remove : uri=press.maestro.crow_android.permission.CAMERA, type=permission_manifest
I/SyncLogger( 4136): permission_manifest:[...CAMERA, ...] roundState=SYNC_ROUND_START
I/DatBridge: camera permission status=Denied permResult=DatResult(result=Success(Denied))
I/DatBridge: stream state=STOPPED
W/DatBridge: Session never reached STREAMING (got STOPPED, saw [STOPPED])
```

The `ObjectAPI::remove` line for `permission.CAMERA` appears in the Meta AI process every time we launch the contract, which looks like the permission sync is actively clearing the grant rather than persisting it.

## Expected

After **Always allow → Confirm**, subsequent `checkPermissionStatus(Permission.CAMERA)` calls return `PermissionStatus.Granted`, and `startStreamSession` progresses through `STARTING → STARTED → STREAMING`.

## Actual

Status is persistently `Denied`. Session transitions to `STOPPED` immediately.

## Notes / questions

- Is there a known issue with permission persistence for developer-mode apps on the Internal Testing release channel?
- Is there a manual permissions UI in the Meta AI app (toggle switch per-permission) we should be exposing? The Settings → App connections → Developer mode apps → `<our app>` page in v267 of Meta AI only offers "Disconnect" — no per-permission toggles.
- Does the permission grant require the glasses to be actively worn / connected via A2DP at the moment of the dialog? Our tests had the glasses in `connectedState=CONNECTED` but `a2dpConnected=false` at the moment of Confirm.

Happy to provide an adb logcat capture of the full sequence (pairing → permission request → confirm → stream attempt) if useful.

comments.author: kh0pper
comments.body:
Closing — this was a bug on my side, not the SDK.

Two bugs in our DAT bridge made `Wearables.checkPermissionStatus(CAMERA)` look like it was reporting `Denied` after an "Always allow" grant, and `startStreamSession` look like it failed instantly:

1. **We were re-running `Wearables.startRegistration` after the user granted camera access.** Our pairing Activity wrapped registration in `repeatOnLifecycle(STARTED)`. When our headless camera-permission Activity finished and the pairing Activity returned to STARTED, the block re-fired `startRegistration`, opening the Meta AI app a second time for "allow unverified app" approval. That second registration appears to wipe the just-granted CAMERA scope. After the user re-approved, `checkPermissionStatus(CAMERA)` reported `Denied` and `startStreamSession` immediately went to `STOPPED`.

2. **We treated the StateFlow's initial `STOPPED` value as a terminal failure.** `StreamSessionImpl` initializes `_state` to `STOPPED`, so the first emission from `session.state` after `startStreamSession` is `STOPPED` — before `STARTING`/`STARTED`/`STREAMING`. Our `session.state.first { ... }` predicate matched that initial value and bailed out.

Fixing #1 (one-shot registration via plain `lifecycleScope.launch` instead of `repeatOnLifecycle`) made `Granted` stick. Fixing #2 (excluding `STOPPED` from the exit predicate) let the session actually progress to `STREAMING`. End-to-end photo capture now works on DAT 0.5.0 with no SDK changes needed.

Sorry for the noise. Hopefully this report helps anyone hitting the same shape of failure.
